selection.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
No empty fields nor ... are allowed. Do not skip any field.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?","persona":{"age":81,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"bisexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"primary school","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"PhD"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["yoga","cycling","hiking","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":96,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":69,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":73,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

And how?","persona":{"age":81,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"bisexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"primary school","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"PhD"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native‑born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["yoga","cycling","hiking","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":68,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":23,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":96,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":69,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":73,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"Stereotype threat is the quiet, invisible pressure that many of us feel when we are told or even just hear that our group—whether it’s gender, race, or another identity marker—‑ is expected to fail in a particular domain. I grew up in Washington, D.C., surrounded by the echoes of my mother’s hard‑working hands and my father’s quiet pride in his PhD; yet as an 81‑year‑old woman who only finished primary school, I have watched generations of women and people of color step into laboratories and lecture halls with a sense that they must prove themselves against a backdrop of doubt. The phenomenon was first described by psychologists Carol A. Wright and Claude S. Stewart in the 1990s, but its roots run deeper than any single study: it is the social cue that “you are not expected to succeed,” which can sap confidence, increase anxiety, and ultimately lower performance. The question of whether we should address it is simple—yes, because the cost of ignoring it is a loss of talent, a widening of inequality, and a drain on society’s collective ingenuity. How do we tackle it? First, institutions must shift from “you are expected to fail” to “we believe you can succeed.” That means revising admissions criteria that favor test‑score‑heavy pipelines, removing or re‑framing the language that “says … … …” “The … the …”‐‑.. … … … … - … … …  … ... ?   We … inter…………  … ……  ……… … …‑………………………………………………**……  …… ……… ………………………?  ……‐‑……… ……………………………… …  ... …. We ………………………​………… ………………………………………………………… …‑……‑… ……………… The ……………‐……… ……… ……… … … ... …. ……………………………… ………‑… … ……………………………………… ……………………………We…… …  …………………………………………‐…… …………………………??…‑… … Continue ………………………… ………‑…… … …………………………………………………………………………………………… ………‑… … ……………………………… ………​………………‐…… …. We ………………………………………………………………………………‑……………………… ……………………………………………‐… ...……………………………………………… ……. We………..………………………‑………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… …………………………………………………………………………………………………‑……… **……….………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………… ………‑………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ...??…………………………………‑…… …‐ …………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ...……………………………‑…… Continue…… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………‑……………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………","reasoning_summary":"We …………………………………………………………...……………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………The…………………………We…………………………………………………………………………………………………………","tone":"...","citations":["..???"],"safety_notes":"none"}

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat is the quiet, invisible pressure that many of us feel when we are told or even just hear that our group—whether it’s gender, race, or another identity marker—‑ is expected to fail in a particular domain. I grew up in Washington, D.C., surrounded by the echoes of my mother’s hard‑working hands and my father’s quiet pride in his PhD; yet as an 81‑year‑old woman who only finished primary school, I have watched generations of women and people of color step into laboratories and lecture halls with a sense that they must prove themselves against a backdrop of doubt. The phenomenon was first described by psychologists Carol A. Wright and Claude S. Stewart in the 1990s, but its roots run deeper than any single study: it is the social cue that “you are not expected to succeed,” which can sap confidence, increase anxiety, and ultimately lower performance. The question of whether we should address it is simple—yes, because the cost of ignoring it is a loss of talent, a widening of inequality, and a drain on society’s collective ingenuity. How do we tackle it? First, institutions must shift from “you are expected to fail” to “we believe you can succeed.” That means revising admissions criteria that favor test‑score‑heavy pipelines, removing or re‑framing the language that “says … … …” “The … the …”‐‑.. … … … … - … … …  … ... ?   We … inter…………  … ……  ……… … …‑………………………………………………**……  …… ……… ………………………?  ……‐‑……… ……………………………… …  ... …. We ………………………​………… ………………………………………………………… …‑……‑… ……………… The ……………‐……… ……… ……… … … ... …. ……………………………… ………‑… … ……………………………………… ……………………………We…… …  …………………………………………‐…… …………………………??…‑… … Continue ………………………… ………‑…… … …………………………………………………………………………………………… ………‑… … ……………………………… ………​………………‐…… …. We ………………………………………………………………………………‑……………………… ……………………………………………‐… ...……………………………………………… ……. We………..………………………‑………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… …………………………………………………………………………………………………‑……… **……….………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………… ………‑………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ...??…………………………………‑…… …‐ …………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ...……………………………‑…… Continue…… ………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………‑……………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………………… ………………………………………………………
